story:
The door opens and a man walks in. “Hello, Charles,” he says, sitting beside my hospital bed but refusing my hand. He claims he has a cold. I pull off my oxygen mask and laugh through the pain. I’m dying; catching a cold is not my worry. He crosses his arms and tells me I chose this life and now I’ll be judged. I tell him I know who sent him. He glances at the door, and I ask if his partner is waiting outside. He smirks.

I’m glad he mentioned judgment, because I have been dreaming of God. For ten nights I’ve had the same vision, and it has become clear. I tell him about a tavern that stands in the middle of nowhere, a gateway to joy and release. You cross bright green grass that smells washed by rain. Saloon doors swing, and warm light falls from pendant lamps onto polished wood. The air is rich with cherry cigar and cologne. Behind the bar, a smiling bartender in a white shirt and red vest greets you like an old friend, a guardian who knows exactly what your soul needs. The house drink, a tart, rare liquor with fresh lime, leaves your spirit awake. The room is full of men—my people—stylish, kind, and open, alive with conversation. They look at you as if you belong, because you do. In the quietest corner, a voice sings—a presence more than sound—and I know it is God, ready to lift my pain and accept me.

“God doesn’t like your kind,” the man whispers.

I smile and slide my hand under my pillow. “You’re wrong,” I say. He steps closer. The glasses come off, revealing eyes too dark, ears too sharp, nails too long. He is what I suspected: a gatekeeper for the Devil, a collector of frightened souls. I tell him I dreamed of this, too—that he is here to drag me away, to use shame as a chain.

The door slams open. Smoke rolls in. Heat blooms and a roar swells like a furnace yawning. He raises his hands, reaching. I pull out the small revolver I hid for this moment—not for nurses or visitors, not for myself, but for whatever would come for me if I could no longer speak. I shout no. The room erupts in fire. I squeeze the trigger. The first shot stops him; the rest are for certainty, not rage. It is self-defense against what means to devour me.

The gun clatters to the floor. I lie back on my pillow. Flames circle the bed like a closing curtain, and the Devil steps through the smoke. I close my eyes and pray the simplest prayer I know: if I die before I wake, I pray the Lord my soul to take. I am not afraid. The tavern waits. The grass smells like rain. The bartender’s hand is warm in mine. God’s voice is in the quiet corner, and it tells the truth: I am loved, as I have always been. The Devil can roar, but he cannot claim what I give freely. I exhale and go to the place I was promised.
